coredata.dc:title: Process intensification for gram-scale synthesis of N-doped carbon quantum dots immersing a microplasma jet in a gas-liquid reactor
coredata.dc:creator: Pho, Quoc Hue
Lin, LiangLiang
Rebrov, Evgeny V.
Mohsen Sarafraz, Mohammad
Tran, Thanh Tung
Tran, Nam Nghiep
Losic, Dusan
Hessel, Volker
coredata.dc:description: N-doped carbon quantum dots are synthesised by immersing a microplasma jet into a gas–liquid reactor of the size of a microwell, containing an aqueous solution of folic acid (Vitamin B9). The distance of the tip of the microplasma jet to the water surface is changed in three steps, named distant, contact, and deflection modes. As a further variation, the liquid volume is either stirred or unstirred and may contain glass beads or metal flakes. In this way, the mass transfer, hydrodynamics, and the electrical field are influenced and create the specific gas–liquid interface, possibly including plasma-catalytic effects. A thermofluidic analysis confirms a uniform temperature profile and a positive temperature effect on the mass transfer. In this way, the research achieves process intensification, bringing the synthesis towards 1 g per day and maximising the intended performance, the photoluminescence intensity. Recycling further increases the mass yield via centrifugation. An analysis by optical emission spectroscopy reveals the formation of the plasma species from which a reaction mechanism is proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Microplasma jet
Gas–liquid reactor
Process intensification
Gram-scales
Vitamin B9

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Suitability of performance and composition to applications of the quantum dots
Mechanistic analysis of the multi-phase process intensification

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Various plasma-liquid interaction systems for nanomaterials synthesis with non-dispersive or dispersive multi-phase interaction: (A) “Hollow-cathode” microplasma system; (B) Microfluidic plasma system; (C) Needle-to-needle plasma system; (D) Wire-to-plane plasma system.
Reactor configurations and experimental synthesis process setup of N-doped CDs.

A: Generic configurations of the microplasma jet-liquid system as a function of the distance between the jet and liquid phase. B: Various operational modes of kINPen®IND ®IND jet-based synthesis reactors. Glass beads (100 µm), metal flakes (copper-nickel) (500 µm), stirring bar (1000 rpm).
Variation of the thermal conductivity (red line) and heat capacity of the argon (blue line) with plasma gas temperature calculated via Uniquac thermodynamic models.

UV–vis (A) and photoluminescence (B) of N-doped carbon dots synthesised for different operational modes, i.e. partly penetrating the liquid volume (deflection mode, kINPen®IND microplasma jet, 25 °C, 0.1 g/mL folic acid solution, 0.5 g metal flakes, 99.99 % Ar gas). Photoluminescence comparison of metal flakes and stirring mode at various positions of microplasma jet (C).

A: The as-prepared N-doped carbon dots in solid-state and liquid-state; and their photoluminescence 3D colour at 1 µg/mL. B, C: EDX elemental analysis and elemental distribution (HAADF) of N-doped carbon dots after 20 min synthesis (deflection mode, metal flakes, kINPen®IND microplasma jet, 25 °C, 0.1 g/mL folic acid solution, 0.5 g metal flakes, 99.99 % Ar gas).

Photoluminescence of N-doped carbon dots at different reaction times (a) and various concentrations (b) at emission wavelength 450 nm (deflection mode, kINPen®IND microplasma jet, 25 °C, 0.1 g/mL folic acid solution, 0.5 g metal flakes, 99.99 % Ar gas).

Photoluminescence of N-doped carbon dots after 10 times of Recycling. Deflection mode, metal flakes (deflection mode, kINPen®IND microplasma jet, 25 °C, 0.1 g/mL folic acid solution, 0.5 g metal flakes, 99.99 % Ar gas).
Chemistry reactions in the purification of N-doped carbon dots using dialysis and precipitation technique.

A: OES comparison of N-doped carbon dots reaction for the processing of immersing an argon plasma jet into the N-doped carbon dots precursor solution with metal flakes at different positions, showing the plasma only (‘Argon’) and showing both plasma and solution-based excited carbon and other species (‘Metal + Solution’). The two other spectra serve for comparison and are explained in the text. B: Analysis of radical species of N-doped carbon dots synthesis (deflection mode, kINPen®IND microplasma jet, 25 °C, 0.1 g/mL folic acid solution, 0.5 g metal flakes, 99.99 % Ar gas).
Proposed mechanism of the microplasma synthesis of N-doped CDs.
Morphology of N-doped CDs with a multi-layer structure characterised by HRTEM.

head.ce:title: Process intensification for gram-scale synthesis of N-doped carbon quantum dots immersing a microplasma jet in a gas-liquid reactor

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Advanced Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon quantum dots are synthesised by immersing a microplasma jet into a gas–liquid reactor of the size of a microwell, containing an aqueous solution of folic acid (Vitamin B9). The distance of the tip of the microplasma jet to the water surface is changed in three steps, named distant, contact, and deflection modes. As a further variation, the liquid volume is either stirred or unstirred and may contain glass beads or metal flakes. In this way, the mass transfer, hydrodynamics, and the electrical field are influenced and create the specific gas–liquid interface, possibly including plasma-catalytic effects. A thermofluidic analysis confirms a uniform temperature profile and a positive temperature effect on the mass transfer. In this way, the research achieves process intensification, bringing the synthesis towards 1 g per day and maximising the intended performance, the photoluminescence intensity. Recycling further increases the mass yield via centrifugation. An analysis by optical emission spectroscopy reveals the formation of the plasma species from which a reaction mechanism is proposed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The key issue around plasma catalysis, or any multi-phase plasma processing, is the short lifetime of the active species [1,2]. This hampers the effectiveness of any conventional matter transport from one phase to the other, even when intensified. At best, the plasma phase is in direct contact with the catalyst or other phase, rendering superfluous any matter transport. Yet that may deteriorate the catalyst by thermal action or cause abrasion, and even then, a major part of the plasma phase will remain as unused bulk volume [3]. Rather than using a pure plasma gas phase, a solution may add a liquid volume and benefit from plasma-multiphase interactions. The liquid volume may be the only reaction medium towards a catalyst (heterogeneous or homogeneous) or contain a reactant molecule (to be converted with or without a catalyst). Here, we consider a gas–liquid reaction without a catalyst or with metal particles of potential catalytic nature.

It is known that plasma-liquid interaction can bring additional benefits compared to pure gas-phase plasmas. Those benefits are enhanced chemical diversity (e.g·H2O2, OH‧, H2O+, H*, etc.), increased fluidic cooling rate, wider precursor choice, and reduced nanoparticle aggregation [4]. The high heat capacity of a liquid phase would likely avoid the thermal hassle (ensuring good thermal transport).

Concerning the distant (non-penetrating) contact mode, one class of plasma gas–liquid reactors have been developed, with applications ranging from nanomaterials synthesis and surface modification to wastewater treatment, VOC decomposition, and antibacterial purpose. Several design variations of this concept are known. For instance, a hollow-cathode microplasma system was proposed by Sankaran et al. for the rapid synthesis of colloidal metal nanoparticles in aqueous solutions (Fig. 1 A) [5,6]. A similar method was also applied to prepare silver nanoparticles and their coverage with a surfactant [7]. Julien et al. used a microfluidic chip to generate plasma within microchannels to evaluate the safety and selectivity of an oxidation reaction converting liquid cyclohexane to form a mixture of cyclohexanol, cyclohexanone and cyclohexyl hydroperoxide [8]. Lin et al. fabricated gold nanoparticles using a one-pot microplasma-induced approach, applying the obtained products for surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopic detection of methylene blue [9]. By integrating plasma-liquid interactions with microfluidics in a T-type capillary, they further synthesised Au-Ag nanoalloys with tunable composition (Fig. 1B) [10].

Another class of plasma gas–liquid reactor with more vigorous contact can cause gas dispersion (e.g. by jet penetration), thus forming the discharges within the bulk liquid. A “needle-to-needle” plasma-liquid interaction configuration was proposed by Jung et al. to fabricate tin and tin oxide (Fig. 1 C). Through process control over pH, plasma exposure time, and precursor concentration, the size and composition of the products can be flexibly tuned [11]. Another plasma-liquid system is reported by Suehiro et al. [12], where plasma discharges are formed between two electrodes (wire-to-plane system) immersed in water, used for carbon nanotube surface modification (Fig. 1 D). Due to the formation of strongly oxidative species like H2O2, OH‧, O‧ to the surface of the carbon nanotubes, their water solubility was significantly enhanced.

Gas-liquid plasma equipment with gas-dispersion is even commercially available, such as the Leap100 reactor from Plasma-Leap Technologies. This is a non-thermal plasma bubble column reactor of dual reactor configurations with multiple discharge schemes and dynamic bubble control [13].This scalable reactor works in continuous mode and has shown a high production rate of ammonia [13], organic pollutant degradation [14], food sanitisation [15], and inactivation of tumourcells [16].

In this research, however, we like to perform fundamental investigations, which follow the principles of chemical reactor design and allow us to make some critical variations easily. Therefore, we have chosen to use simple equipment, the commercially available KINPen®IND reactor, providing a cold atmospheric-pressure microplasma jet with noble gases such as argon. The KINPen®IND microplasma jet reactor has found large usage and considerable literature documentation [17–22].

This paper explores the KINPen®IND microplasma jet reactor to synthesise N-doped carbon nanodots from folic acid (Vitamin B9) in an aqueous solution. We like to revisit processing with it from the chemical engineering viewpoint. This concern mainly the interaction of the jet with a liquid volume (which may contain a catalyst). Our preliminary work shows that metal flakes can act as an electrocatalyst in the decomposition of folic acid. To the best of our knowledge, the inner electrons of the alloys tend to move from nickel to copper in the electrochemical series. In this case, Cu acts as a strong oxidant (electron receptors) while Ni acts as a reduction (electron donors). As a result, more reactive electrons are on the surface of the catalysts. When electrons and ionised species from microplasma colloid with the surface of the catalysts, the electrons on the surface can be more reactive and removed from the surface to colloid with folic acid molecules. As a result, it causes more folic acid molecules to be decomposed.

By varying the jet-liquid distance in this way, the plasma jet can be turned vigorous in its interaction with a liquid volume due to its high velocities, which can induce turbulence in small liquid volumes, facilitating mass transport. In that mode, the liquid surface is constantly deformed and renewing. In the strongest interaction, it is expected that plasma gas bubbles may form with a favourably small interface for mass transfer. The latter effect would also increase residence time, besides mass transfer. As an option, we load the liquid volume with solid particles, increasing the mixing effect by impulse transfer and may even act as reaction-promoting material. The latter might also happen by changing the electric field when the particles are electrically conductive.

The reactor configuration and experimental synthesis process was adapted from our previous study [24]. In brief, the synthesis reactors include two parts: (1) a kINPen®IND microplasma jet with a dimension of 105 × 180 × 330 mm (H × W × D) was purchased from Neoplas GmbH, Germany and (2) an open glass reactor was designed and fabricated in our laboratory with a 9 mm × 15 mm (W × H) dimension. A 110 VAC power supply (50 Hz) was used to connect with the kINPen®IND microplasma jet. A continuous Ar (99.9 %) gas stream was connected to the microplasma jet. A stable microplasma stream with a length of 1 cm was formed under an Ar gas stream (2 sccm) and a regulated pressure of 3 Bar. To understand the reaction mechanism, an optical fibre was set up in the plasma-liquid interaction region to detect reactive species generated from microplasma. The general synthesis process was also described in Fig. 2 . Briefly, folic acid (FA) solution (0.1 g/mL) as a starting carbon source was prepared by dissolving 2 g of folic acid (98 %) in 20 ml of NaHCO3 (0.5 M) as a solvent under stirring until the resulting orange solution was homogeneously mixed. 1 ml of FA solution (0.1 mg/mL) was then transferred to the open reactor and then treated under microplasma.

To operate in the non-dispersive and dispersive mode, we chose three generic configurations of positioning the plasma jet relative to the liquid surface ( Fig. 3
A). In the “distant” position, we emulate many plasma reactor configurations reported in the literature, following the design shown in Fig. 1 A. The most eminent part of the microplasma jet (assumed to be the tip) is brought in contact with the liquid surface. One would assume hardly any impact on the liquid phase hydrodynamics and mass transfer. In the “contact” position, we emulate the high-intensity gas–liquid reactors of Wild et al. This may force the adjacent region of the liquid phase into motion, yet likely may leave the bulk liquid volume still unaffected. In the “deflection” position, we emulate the Botes plasma reactor configuration of the ‘high velocity impinging stream reactors’. The shape and velocity of microplasma has changed significantly when changing the mode from distant to contact and deflection. The term “contact” here means the tip of the microplasma is in contact right in the bottom of the reactor. The term “deflection” here means the microplasma will reflect back due to convection effect when the distance is shorter than the contact position. By deflecting the plasma jet on the floor of the reactor well, momentum is transferred into the whole liquid bulk volume. The expectation is to generate a highly turbulent system with constant renewal of interfaces, potentially with the generation and breakup of plasma droplets.

In one step further, an experimental set is conducted when glass beads (100 µm) or metal flakes (copper-nickel) (500 µm) are added to the liquid volume or a mixing bar for stirring (1000 rpm) ( Fig. 3 B). In this way, reactor engineering is changed fundamentally. First, mass transfer is enhanced, both by the bar and the particles, which can pick up the impulse of the jet. Second, the plasma may interact with the particles, driven both by their material properties and geometry (size, shape), as known from literature and our investigations. The presence of a material can change the plasma and electric field intensity and presumably the nature and concentration of the plasma excited species.

The recycling and purification process is based on precipitation, centrifugation and dialysis. After the first treatment of folic acid solution under microplasma, the resulting solution is diluted into water and then precipitated by HCl (0.5 M) under stirring. Afterwards, the resulting mixture is separated by centrifugation (4000 rpm) for 30 min. After centrifugation, the supernatant is used for dialysis against water for three days. The residue of unreacted folic acid is washed with water and dissolved back to NaHCO3 for the second synthesis. This process will be repeated ten times of recycling. The dialysed N-doped carbon dots are freeze-dried to obtain solid N-doped carbon dots.

Fluorescence spectra were carried out on a photoluminescence Spectrometer (FLS 1000, United Kingdom). The system is a modular fluorescence and phosphorescence spectrometer for measuring ultraviolet (UV) spectra to the mid-infrared spectral range. Firstly, Photoluminescence 3D colour mapping was investigated to determine the as-prepared N-doped carbon dots' maximal excitation and emission wavelength. The excitation and emission spectra were set from 300 to 700 nm and plotted in photoluminescence 3D colour mapping. After investigating the excitation and emission wavelength, these parameter values are used for further photoluminescence intensity tests. 1 ml of the as-prepared N-doped carbon dots (1 µg/mL) is inserted into the FLS1000 under the investigated excitation light source to determine the photoluminescence curve at the maximal wavelength.

UV–vis spectra are another important optical parameter that needs to investigate. Briefly, 1 ml of the as-prepared N-doped carbon dots (1 µg/mL) is inserted into Cary 60 UV–vis Agilent Technologies to record the spectra to determine the maximal absorbance wavelength. Based on this data, further absorbance tests are carried out.

FEI Quanta 450 FEG Environmental Scanning Electron Microscope (ESEM) ascertained N-doped carbon dots' elemental co mposition and structural purity. Briefly, 10 mg of N-doped carbon dots were dispersed into ethanol under sonication to ensure the N-doped carbon dots dissolved well and homogeneously. Later on, the N-doped carbon dots solution is drop-wised into a carbon tape and put on the ESEM holder. Then, the ESEM sample is dried up in the oven at 50 0C to remove solvents. Finally, the samples are tested with FEI Quanta 450 FEG Environmental Scanning Electron Microscope (ESEM) to record EDX data.

To determine the elemental distribution (HAADF) and HRTEM, the as-prepared N-doped carbon dots were tested with FEI Titan Themis 80–200. Briefly,10 mg of N-doped carbon dots were dispersed into water under sonication. Then, the N-doped carbon dots solution is drop-wised into the TEM grid. Afterwards, the grid is dried up naturally at a certain time. Finally, the prepared sample is tested for elemental distribution (HAADF).

The optical emission spectrum was measured using an HR2000 + ES spectrometer (Ocean Optics, Inc.). An optical fibre outside the reactor collected the emitted light from the plasma gas–liquid interactions.The OES fibre was inserted into the liquid-plasma interaction where the reaction occurs and then transmitted to a computer to record the emission.

Often overlooked in chemical engineering literature is that plasma can promote physical properties and fluid transport. Micro-scale phenomena such as Brownian motion (gas–liquid) [25] and thermophoresis [26] can play a vital role in plasma chemistry. The issue here, using a microplasma jet, is that heat transfer in a multi-phase plasma requires efficient localised and on-spot thermal energy in the form of sensible heat to be released on the surface/inside the folic acid precursor solution. Short-lived reactive species must be created at the right position, ensuring they are reactive enough. These short-living plasma species include but are not limited to spinning, fast-travelling excited species, charged particles, surficial or gas bulk atomic or molecular radicals, and highly reactive molecules. Likewise, Ion species can be traced and measured in the effluent of the kINPen®IND jet microplasma reactor. The calculated quasi-neutral density is ∼ 1012 cm−3 when created by kINPen®IND at 20 W [27]. This is a sufficient density number for fast and highly localised electron impact reactions to generate and drive any surface reactions in the gas/liquid [28]. The kINPen®IND microplasma jet reactor can preheat the plasma up to 70 °C (343 K, Fig. 4 ).

While the gas temperature is less than 313 K, at a frequency of ∼ 1 MHz, the kINPen®IND 's nominal exergy power is approximately 1 W once a power throughput of 20 W is applied to the plasma driver. The discharge pathway from the tip of the KINPen®IND follows the air impurity distribution in the argon jet effluent demonstrated by [27]. The calculated thermal conductivity k and heat capacity Cp for argon in the kINPen®IND microplasma jet are given as a function of temperature (Fig. 5 ). When increasing the temperature from 298 K to 348 K, k increases by only ∼ 10 %, while Cp changes by 0.1 %.

As represented in Fig. 4, the heat capacity (Cp) value slightly changes with the temperature of the plasma gas (0.4 %), which in turn shows that there is no non-uniformity in the spatiotemporal temperature profile of the plasma gas due to the variation in the heat capacity and sensible heat gradient. It is also worth mentioning that the reactivity of the plasma is a strong function of the distribution and formation of the radical species, which is spatially non-uniform and scattered within the reactor. Accordingly, as opposed to conventional reactors, in plasma, the only variation in the energy profile is sourced from radical species rather than sensible heat transfer. Notably, conventional reactors (e.g., continuous stirred-tank reactor, CSTR) have technical challenges associated with the relatively slow kinetics (magnitude of few seconds) compared to plasma (magnitude of microseconds). Also important is the formation of dead zones that suppress the chemical performance of the reactors; however, in plasma jets, there is no dead zone formation as the reactor’s gap is a few millimetres.

Concerning the thermal conductivity, the second positive finding is that the kINPen®IND reactor is not energy-intensive but is process-intensive. The power source is used to excite plasma chemistry and, to an extent, increase transport properties by increasing the thermal conductivity. Improving the thermal conductivity is necessary for better chemical performance, faster response, and faster reactions. This is because improving the thermal conductivity of a gas is proportional to its thermal diffusivity. The thermal diffusivity is defined as the ratio of thermal conductivity (k) to the products of gas bulk density and heat capacity, referred to as
α =
(
k
ρ
c p
). The value of
α
is enormously intensified by increasing thermal conductivity and accordingly affects the time required for the plasma reactor to reflect the effect of the temperature. The higher the thermal diffusivity, the smaller the response time and more uniform thermal transport. This clarifies that the plasma chemistry of argon at T < 343 K is coping with reaction characteristics of the fast plasma (microseconds) and is energetically symmetric and uniform.

The following experiments were made with this contact concept to conclude that the deflection mode is most effective for mass transfer in terms of turbulence and plasma volume extension. The question is if the plasma jet, now able to penetrate the liquid surface partly, can stir the liquid volume enough to avoid mass transfer limitation. Thus, we installed a benchmark experiment with a mixing bar. We used glass beads to intensify the jet impulse for mixing (and to be able to form a trickle bed at a high filling degree). Metal flakes served the same purpose but with the additional hope that they might act as catalysts for the nanoreaction and/or modify the electrical field.

Rather than analysing the quantum dots’ size, morphology, crystallinity, etc., we take photoluminescence as a gross performance parameter, which plays a crucial role in applying carbon quantum does for wastewater purification and health care (theranostic agents). We ranked the different plasma gas–liquid reactor configurations in their reaction performance with these metrics.

Fig. 5A shows the UV–vis spectra of the different reactor configurations. A steady increase in absorption is observed from the processing without stirring, using the impulse transfer to the (inert) glass beads, mixing with a bar and metal flakes. When reviewing photoluminescence, the same order of impact is confirmed, yet showing a much stronger effect of the last reactor concept, i.e. the use of metal flakes. It seems that stirring the liquid volume is advantageous. Likely, the mixing bar is more effective than the jet impulse transfer to the glass beads, which might be too heavy and large for the effect chosen. Thus, it might be doubtful if the metal flake effect is alone due to a stirring effect, as metal flakes and glass beads have similar size and mass. Instead, it might be guessed if the flakes act catalytically or interact with the plasma-electrical field. An indication of that is the liquid solution's darkening, forming during the plasma exposure and reaction.

Fig. 5B shows that the deflection mode is better than the contact or distant mode. In terms of photoluminescence, the performance outcome is triple compared to the distant mode. This demonstrates that learning from high-intensification gas–liquid processing is effective for microplasma gas–liquid processing. Fig. 5C also confirms that using metal flakes is superior to stirring throughout; because of impact-driven mixing and a possible catalytic-electric effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Suitability of performance and composition to applications of the quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Process intensification of quantum materials does not end in achieving a synthesis with a maximised product output; rather, maximising the performance of the nanomaterial must be the goal. Nanomaterials are ‘performance chemicals’ in the sense of specialty chemicals, and customers are willing to pay for their function and not for their mass.

In this lieu, Fig. 6
A demonstrates the performance of the obtained N-doped carbon dots in terms of their main functional parameter, photoluminescence. The obtained emission wavelengths of 454 nm, absorbance wavelength at 310 nm, and excitation wavelength of 360 nm suit applications in wastewater processing regarding photocatalysis [29,30], nanopesticides [31] and health theranostics [32] regarding photoluminescence.

Photocatalysis and photoluminescence of N-doped carbon dots are “golden properties” that make it widely applicable in wastewater processing and medical theranostics. With regard to the former application, CDs play a role in the photocatalytic degradation of organic compounds or microorganisms [29,33–35]. N-doped carbon dots absorb the light at a wavelength of 310 nm to generate e−/h+ pairs. Under the excitation of 360 nm, e-/h+ pairs are separated and transferred to generate reactive oxygen species. Finally, the ROS groups cause subsequent photocatalytic reactions to decompose the structure of organic compounds or microorganisms [36,37].

Regarding the latter, N-doped carbon dots with surfaces modified by receptors such as folate will internalise into tumour cells [35,38]. Then, CDs exhibit strong blue fluorescence under suitable illumination when imaging tumour cells [39–41]. In parallel, ROS groups are also generated from N-doped carbon dots under UV exposure to kill tumour cells [39,42,43]. Many studies have used C-dots for bioimaging under UV irradiation. For example, Smriti Sri et al. synthesized zwitterionic carbon dots as a novel approach for bioimaging applications in cancerous cells [44]. In addition, many studies reported that N-doped CDs could be used as a nanocarrier to load drugs as a therapeutic medicine [45]. Therefore, the authors believe that N-doped CDs can be utilised for theranostic applications under UV irradiation. The as-prepared N-doped CDs can emit a blue fluorescence (454 nm) under an excitation of 360 nm. This indicates that our N-doped CDs can be excited under (near UV) UVA, which is less harmful than UVB and UVC.

A point beam EDX analysis of N-doped carbon dots in Fig. 6 B shows that the C-represents only three significant peaks of carbon (44.6 %), oxygen (29.8 %), and nitrogen (25.5 %) in terms of atomic mass. This indicates that the as-prepared N-doped carbon dots are highly pure without the elemental signature of any foreign impurities other than carbon and oxygen, and nitrogen. Several peaks are not labelled in the EDX spectra. These peaks are attributed to the substrate, which is carbon tape. In the chemical composition of carbon tape, except for carbon which is the main component, silicon and aluminium are also found. For this reason, several peaks have been detected in the EDX spectra. Regarding our purification methodology, the mixed solution includes N-doped CDs, NaCl, and H2O before dialysis. Therefore, the EDX spectra in our study show no presence of NaCl, and the phrase “the elemental signature of any foreign impurities other than carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen” means that no NaCl was found after dialysis.

While not quantitative, the elemental distribution given in Fig. 6 C shows that all elements are distributed relatively uniform in the nanomaterial. C and N are the expected, name-giving constituents, and O is an unwanted side-constituent. Still, a spread in colour intensity is visible. For example, the left-down side of the C elemental distribution is more intense, maybe because of a higher quantum dot thickness rather than a higher presence of carbon. It is also evident that nitrogen and oxygen have lower contents than carbon, in a similar amount, yet seemingly with somewhat more oxygen than nitrogen. This result also agrees with EDX, confirming the high purity of the as-prepared N-doped carbon dots. Apart from the above characterisation, the XRD, XPS, FTIR, and 1H NMR reported from our previous studies revealed that the core structure of N-doped CDs was in the form of a crystalline with multilayered graphitic- and pyrrolic-nitrogen defected graphene sheets. The surface of N-doped CDs was decorated by –NH2, –OH, and –COOH functional groups. The resulting N-doped CDs had an intensely blue fluorescence performance with a QY of 35 % [46].

Having identified that the deflection mode and addition of metal flakes are best for the quantum dot formation, a small reaction engineering study shall reveal the productivity of the reaction concept and if process intensification is found, which is usually synonymously quoted with the use of emerging technologies. Process intensification means maximising the performance obtained, either relative to the same plasma reactor system or other technologies giving the same quantum dot product, with the ambition to be significantly better, e.g. order of magnitude.

The approach is trifold. i) operating at best kinetical parameters by varying reaction time and concentration; ii) determining mass reaction efficiency of best conditions; iii) increasing reaction mass efficiency by recycling and repeated reaction with the same starting material.

Photoluminescence intensity is the performance considered, relating to a high mass yield of our plasma process. A molecular yield cannot be defined for a nanomaterial (having no molecular mass). Rather than making a comprehensive study in an optimisation fashion, we focus here on varying two key process parameters, which are the reaction time and reactant concentration, as central process engineering parameters. Thereafter, we perform process optimisation by varying the process itself, adding agitation and reaction-promoting materials to the blank plasma gas–liquid process.

Fig. 7 plots the emitted fluorescence (photoluminescence) intensity, which is equivalent to nanodot concentration, which is a function of reaction time; the concentration scales with the mass yield as we keep the reaction volume constant. Fig. 7A shows a linear increase in the fluorescence intensity when processing from 0 to 20 min. After that, the emitted photoluminescence intensity grows hardly, reaching saturation. At 20 min, about 90 % of the maximal photoluminescence intensity is reached. Thus a chemical manufacturing process would likely be operated here, as any other time would have a lower rate (while giving a little bit more concentration/mass yield). This classifies the microplasma jet process as fast according to the terminology of process intensification, and the reaction time is similarly short as for typical flow chemistry processes. The productivity of our laboratory microwell amounts to 51 mg/ hour (after purification), noting that it levels off after 20 min, and the system would have to be recharged with fresh material. Several papers quote nanomaterial mass yields in that order (lower than 1 g) as scale-up syntheses on the way to production. Thus, with the simple arrangement we have (for fundamental studies), the productivity of 1 g per day could be achieved, and more by parallelisation (in the Conclusions, we outline this is then better done in a continuous-flow system).

It has to be noted that in diluted concentration, the relationship between fluorescence intensity and nanodot mass is assumed to be linear. In high concentration, however, the relation between photoluminescence and concentration is non-linear due to the inner filter effect by quenching and the absorbance of fluorescence light.

Fig. 7B shows a linear dependency of the fluorescence intensity reaching a plateau. In a most simple approximation, one would expect dependence on the concentration of the reactant folic acid, as the plasma reaction is a bimolecular reaction between a primary plasma excited species and folic acid as the N-doped carbon dots precursor. The plateau at high concentration then could be due to the N-doped carbon dots precursor being in large excess compared to the primary plasma excited species, meaning the reaction becomes quasi-monomolecular. An alternative explanation is that mass transfer reaches the maximal value due to the saturation of the gas–liquid interface.

To add the sustainability viewpoint, we consider mass efficiency as a green chemistry metric [47,48], which normalises the mass of the product (nanomaterials) by dividing it with the mass of the reactant (folic acid); for the purified material as shown in the Eq. (1). The productivity of our N-doped CDs was calculated after purification (dialysis). The reaction mass efficiency for the above-given reaction conditions is 17 %, which is very high for a nanomaterial product. It means that after 20 min of treatment under microplasma, the solution was purified by dialysis to obtain 17 mg N-doped CDs out of 100 mg of folic acid. Those or related efficiencies are usually much lower and rarely mentioned in nanomaterials literature. In a recent paper, we overviewed the literature efficiencies of diverse nanomaterial processes, ranging between 0.44 % and 30 % [49]. In a process close to the one reported here, Ma et al. obtained 0.42 % carbon quantum dot efficiency from 1 g citric acid and 0.6 ml ethylenediamine using a self-designed microplasma reactor. In a process different from that reported here, Kurniawan et al. used a direct current (dc) Ar microplasma electrochemical reactor to obtain a production rate of 40 mg/h and conversion rate of 50 % of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots [50]. Therefore, the results show that using folic acid in the microplasma process can significantly enhance mass reaction efficiency. With this synthesis productivity, it is believed that 1 g of N-doped can be achieved in a day with just a single microplasma jet. Another idea proposed to tackle gram-scale production is that an array of microplasma jets can be installed to increase the productivity of N-doped CDs in an energy-efficient, eco-friendly, and safe manner.

It has been considered that fragmentation of the precursor molecule is inherent to any plasma process, meaning there is a share of recombination and inherent loss of material due to species that do not recombine. That has to be considered when referring to conversion(reactant)-based efficiency, while ours is yield (product)-based. This spills over to the question of how effective recycling can be and if all starting materials can be utilised.

We have previously reported the circular economy on nanomaterials comparing diverse syntheses, which pointed out the importance of gaining a high mass yield [49]. We concluded that thermal processes can significantly enhance the mass yield of N-doped carbon dots and possibly to an industrial scale. However, the sustainability and environmentally friendliness of N-doped carbon dots synthesis processes, as well as the reuse of unreacted starting materials, need to be considered here.

With this study, we can put our experimental results in the context of the reported assessment of the diverse syntheses on nanomaterials. We employ vitamin B9, which is a high-prized precursor material. While the mass reaction efficiency of 17 % of our process is excellent compared to state-of-the-art, it nonetheless demands a repeated synthesis with the separated N-doped carbon dots precursor material for gaining sustainability. In this sense, we have studied the quality of the formed N-doped carbon dots from recycled precursor solutions.

We have investigated ten cycles, and found that we get reasonable functional performance, as measured by photoluminescence, for six cycles; see Fig. 8 . In this way, we could increase the mass reaction efficiency by 26 %; yet we admittedly have losses in the recycle loops.

The results indicate that the intensity of photoluminescence of N-doped carbon dots reduces to 50 % at the 6th recycle and to approximately 90 % at the 10th recycle. Apparently, a large amount of unreacted FA remains after each synthesis step. Obviously, the reactive plasma species cannot reach the whole amount of FA for a multi-phase process, with mass transfer hindrance at the interface.

Our purification method assumes that only folic acid (Vitamin B9) dissolves in NaHCO3 solution, while none of the nanomaterials produced thereof (Fig. 9 ). This is acid-base precipitation with folic acid. Fig. 9 describes the chemistry reactions from pre-synthesis to the purification of N-doped carbon dots. Firstly, folic acid reacts with sodium hydrocarbonate to form sodium folate solution in excess sodium hydrocarbonate solution. After microplasma treatment, N-doped carbon dots were obtained along with excess sodium hydrocarbonate, unreacted sodium folate, and water. Afterwards, the resulting mixture was neutralised with HCl to obtain the precipitate of unreacted folic acid, sodium chloride, and resulting N-doped carbon dots. After centrifugation, the resulting mixture is divided into two parts: unreacted folic acid (1) and a mixture of N-doped carbon dots along with sodium and water (2). In part (1), the mixture is dialysed against water to remove sodium chloride. Finally, in part (2), unreacted folic acid is recycled, and pure N-doped carbon dots are obtained in part (1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic analysis of the multi-phase process intensification
ce:section.ce:para: For the best processing, an argon plasma jet was exposed to an aqueous solution containing the N-doped carbon dots precursors and metal flakes and optical emission spectra (OES) to reveal the intermediate plasma species. The spectra were measured at two positions; in the plasma gas phase and at the gas–liquid interface, meaning considering, in the last case, plasma-activated species arising from the solution and by interaction with the metal flakes. Spectra with water or metal flakes only were also monitored for comparison when exposed to the plasma jet.

Fig. 10 A shows that the argon plasma-focused OES graphs are higher in the left-centred group of peaks that belong to the argon-excited species plus excited species from other air components. The middle part of the OES spectrum (from about 400 to about 700 nm) shows the secondary excited species from the carbon-, nitrogen and oxygen-containing precursor of the N-doped carbon dots, which likely are formed through reaction with primary argon excited species. Fig. 10B shows that the presence of metal flakes in solutions significantly increases the presence of the secondary excited species, the N-doped carbon dots metabolites. CH• and:CC: (the C 2 Swan system) fragments dominate for carbon besides O- and H-related species [51,52]. NH• (amino radicals) [53] and •CN (cyano radicals) [54] are found at 336 nm and 379 nm, respectively. •CN radicals are the primary source to form nitrogen defects in the form of graphitic and pyrrolic nitrogen. In contrast, NH• radicals are the fundamental species to introduce –NH2 groups into the surface of N-doped carbon dots.

Without having quantified our analysis, the significant difference between the argon plasma-related peaks and those of the N-doped carbon dots metabolites makes clear that the excited argon species constitute the major component in the observed optical zone. Yet, the N-doped carbon dots metabolites might have a significantly larger share in the liquid phase. The OES camera likely sees only the minute part of the emanated species at and slightly above the interface.

Fig. 10 shows that the presence of the metal flakes largely increases the N-doped carbon dots metabolites compared to the solution-alone processing. Processing with just metal flakes or solution does not show these peaks, as expected, but shows differences in the spectral peaks related to argon, i.e. the primary excited species.

Fig. 11 shows the mechanism of the microplasma synthesis of N-doped CDs, which is in agreement with the exciting species found in the OES analysis ( Fig. 10 ). After FA solution is exposed to microplasma, the chemical structure is decomposed into intermediate reactive species such as CH• [51],:C = CP: [51], •CN [54], OH• [51], NH• [53]. Then, these reactive radicals will react together to nucleate larger radicals, i.e. phenyl, pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen radicals. In the growth stage, phenyl, pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen radicals collide and aggregate to form a single layer of graphene sheet-like structure with pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen defects as well as –NH2 functional groups. Finally, these sheets will gather together layer by layer to form a new stable structure with spherical morphology [55], which is N-doped carbon dots. The morphology and inner structure of N-doped CDs have been characterised by HRTEM. Fig. 12 showed that the as-prepared N-doped CDs have spherical morphology highcrystallinity and multilayered structure with graphene sheets. Theinterlayerspacing of N-doped CDs was calculated byFast Fourier Transform(FFT) from Image J software. The results indicated that theinterlayerspacing between twographene layersin N-doped CDs was 0.27 nm, indicating the (002) plane of the graphite structure.

N-doped carbon quantum dots are synthesised by immersing a microplasma jet into a gas–liquid reactor of the size of a microwell, containing an aqueous solution of folic acid (Vitamin B9). An initial experiment investigated the effect of the distance of the tip of the microplasma jet to the water surface. The closer it came, the more the plasma was deformed and spread out into the whole gaseous reaction volume. In the contact and deflection mode, the jet penetrated the liquid volume down to the reaction well floor. From then onwards, we investigated only the deflection mode. As a further process variation, it was shown that stirred microwell operation delivers higher photoluminescence than unstirred one. The addition of glass beads results in higher photoluminescence, demonstrating the impulse transfer-based mass transfer improvement than the unstirred mode but cannot reach the quality of the stirred plasma operation.

In contrast, adding metal flakes is superior to all modes of operation. It is assumed that this cannot be alone because of better mass transfer and hydrodynamics but may be due to changing the electrical field and possibly plasma-catalytic effects. A thermofluidic analysis confirms a uniform temperature profile and a positive temperature effect on the mass transfer. In this way, the research demonstrated we achieved process intensification. The intended performance for the foreseen applications of the nanomaterial, the photoluminescence intensity, was maximised. In addition, we could reach a productivity of 1 g per day. This is much more than many reported plasma-based and other process-based nanomaterial syntheses in literature and is even quoted to be on the way to production (as nanomaterials most often are only needed in minute volumes). We reported in the paper a synthesis, which in one run makes about 51 mg of product (in 60 min).

Meanwhile, several collaboration partners have requested our material for use in their studies. We have just shipped a sample of about 1 g to India, which is the combined outcome of 20 process runs with about 51 mg each. As this could be elegantly done within one day of work, we think we can claim a gram-scale synthesis if we would work for several days. Recycling further increases the mass yield via precipitation and centrifugation. An analysis by optical emission spectroscopy reveals the formation of the plasma species from which a reaction mechanism is proposed. This analysis also clearly shows the increased reactive excited plasma intermediates when adding the metal flakes.

Thus, we could conduct a comprehensive chemical engineering study, targeting hydrodynamics, heat and mass transfer, and reactivity in the way of traditional reactor engineering and process design. We added the viewpoint of process intensification by following the concept of high-intensity gas–liquid reactors reported in the literature. Such consequent chemical engineering guidance is largely missing in the operation of plasma reactors. We could show the benefit of adding a liquid phase to the plasma gas, besides just carrying a reactant. For better recycling, we propose combining dialysis and precipitation to remove both large and medium-sized particles. Precipitation does remove only large ones.

Quoc Hue Pho: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Liangliang Lin: Writing – review & editing. Mohammad Mohsen Sarafraz: Writing – review & editing. Evgeny V. Rebrov: Writing – review & editing. Thanh Tung Tran: Conceptualization, Writing- Review and Editing. Nam Nghiep Tran: Writing – review & editing. Dusan Losic: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Volker Hessel: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Hue Quoc Pho received the divisional ECMS and full-fee scholarships from the University of Adelaide, Australia. Liangliang Lin thanks the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190605) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22078125, 52004102). All authors acknowledge support from the ERC Grant Surface-Confined fast-modulated Plasma for process and Energy intensification (SCOPE) from the European Commission with grant number 810182.
